section: Effect of Al Content on Inclusions in Fe-15Mn-xAl-C Low-Density Steel

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the abstract, introduction, and start of the experimental section of a study on Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel with Al contents of 1, 3, and 5 wt%. The abstract reports that inclusion types are Al2O3, AlN, MnS, TiN, and their composites; with increasing Al, the number percentage of Al2O3 decreases and AlN increases, and the size distribution shifts toward larger inclusions. FactSage calculations indicate Al raises liquidus, lowers solidus, narrows austenite region, and delays AlN/MnS/TiN precipitation. 2D mismatch values are provided. The introduction surveys literature on Al effects on inclusions in medium/high Mn steels, noting transitions from MnS to AlN dominance, AlN formation in liquid above 3.28% Al, and heterogeneous nucleation of MnS on AlN. The experimental section describes smelting of the first sample: iron powder 700 g, ferrous oxalate 1.575 g, alumina crucible, 1873 K, addition of 225 g ferromanganese followed by 11 g Al wrapped in pure iron, with total Al additions of 28 g and 48 g for the other two samples. Chemical compositions of raw materials are mentioned but the table is not visible yet. Subsequent chunks should provide the full chemical compositions, detailed inclusion characterization (morphology maps, size distributions), and the discussion on solidification routes and nucleation mechanisms based on FactSage and mismatch theory.
material_systems: Inclusion phases: Al2O3, AlN, MnS, TiN, Al2O3-MnS, AlN-MnS, TiN-MnS, Al2O3-AlN-MnS
processing_route: Laboratory smelting in tubular furnace at 1873 K
Raw materials: iron powder, ferrous oxalate, ferromanganese, pure aluminum particles
Sequence: heat iron powder + ferrous oxalate to 1873 K, hold 40 min, add ferromanganese, hold 20 min, add Al wrapped in pure iron sheet, hold 60 min, furnace cool
key_variables: Holding times: 40 min before deoxidation, 20 min after FeMn, 60 min after Al
microstructure: Inclusion types: Al2O3, AlN, MnS, TiN and composites
Trend: higher Al reduces Al2O3 number percentage, increases AlN number percentage
Size shift: fewer 1–5 μm inclusions, more 5–10 μm inclusions with Al
mechanisms: Al increases liquidus, decreases solidus, narrows austenite region, expands ferrite region
Al delays precipitation of AlN, MnS, TiN
2D mismatch favorable for Al2O3/MnS (8.41%), AlN/MnS (8.94%) heterogeneous nucleation
AlN can nucleate on Al2O3 (11.66% mismatch)
figures_or_tables: Table of chemical composition of raw materials (mentioned but not displayed in this chunk)
unresolved_terms: Full details of chemical composition table
Morphology and size distribution data from FE-SEM/EDS

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the smelting procedure details for Fe-15Mn-xAl-C steels with Al additions of 11, 28, and 48 g, including raw material compositions (Table 1) and final steel compositions (Table 2). The three experimental steels are designated 1# (0.868% Al), 2# (2.766% Al), and 3# (4.695% Al). Increasing Al content decreases N from 0.0032% to 0.0021% and slightly increases O from 0.0005% to 0.0013%. The chunk then extensively describes inclusion morphologies and types observed via FE-SEM in Figures 1–3. For 1#: elongated Al2O3 (>8 μm), AlN (~12 μm), angular TiN, and composite inclusions including Al2O3-MnS (inner-outer layers), AlN-MnS, TiN-MnS (clear interfacial relationship), and Al2O3·AlN·MnS 3D morphology. For 2#: single AlN (~23 μm), octahedral MnS, boot-like TiN, Al2O3-MnS, AlN-MnS with close binding, and layered Al2O3-AlN-MnS. For 3#: polyhedral AlN, octahedral MnS (~10 μm), elongated TiN (>30 μm), Al2O3-MnS with Al2O3 at bottom, cigarette-bag shaped TiN-MnS, and very tight non-physical AlN-MnS binding. These observations establish the diversity and evolution of inclusion structures with Al content, which are fundamental for subsequent quantitative analysis and mechanistic discussion.
processing_route: Smelting at 1873 K, holding 40 min before deoxidation, 20 min after 225 g FeMn, 60 min after Al addition (11, 28, 48 g), furnace cooling
microstructure: Inclusion morphologies: elongated, angular, octahedral, polyhedral, boot-like, cigarette-bag
Composite structures: inner-outer layers, close binding, clear interfacial relationship, non-physical tight bonding
Inclusion types observed: Al2O3, AlN, MnS, TiN, composites
unresolved_terms: Quantitative size distributions and number percentages of inclusions

context_update.summary: This chunk first completes inclusion morphology observations (Figure 3h: cigarette-bag TiN-MnS in 3#; Figure 3i: tightly bound AlN-MnS in 3#). It then reports quantitative inclusion statistics: number percentages of various inclusion types (Al2O3, AlN, MnS, TiN, Al2O3-MnS, AlN-MnS, TiN-MnS, Al2O3-AlN-MnS) across samples 1# to 3# (Figure 4), and size distributions (1-5 μm, 5-10 μm, >10 μm) with specific percentages (Figure 5). Clear trends with Al content are given. The discussion begins with FactSage-calculated solidification routes: 1# is fully FCC, while 2# and 3# involve BCC, with liquidus increasing and solidus decreasing as Al rises (Figure 6, Table 3). Using thermodynamic and Clyne-Kurz segregation models, the AlN precipitation solidification fractions are computed as 0.46, 0.22, and 0.51 for 1#, 2#, 3#, respectively, with a non-monotonic shift attributed to differing Al and N segregation and reduced N solubility in 3# (Figures 7 & 8). Key quantitative values, phase evolution paths, and segregation-based precipitation mechanics established here will support subsequent analysis of other inclusions and overall structure-property links.
mechanisms: Al increases liquidus, decreases solidus, promotes BCC phase in solidification
N segregation >> Al segregation controls AlN precipitation timing; decreased N solubility in 3# retards AlN

section: 4.2.1. Effect of Al Content on the Formation of AlN Inclusions
previous_context_summary:
Summary: These observations establish the diversity and evolution of inclusion structures with Al content, which are fundamental for subsequent quantitative analysis and mechanistic discussion. This chunk first completes inclusion morphology observations (Figure 3h: cigarette-bag TiN-MnS in 3#; Figure 3i: tightly bound AlN-MnS in 3#). It then reports quantitative inclusion statistics: number percentages of various inclusion types (Al2O3, AlN, MnS, TiN, Al2O3-MnS, AlN-MnS, TiN-MnS, Al2O3-AlN-MnS) across samples 1# to 3# (Figure 4), and size distributions (1-5 μm, 5-10 μm, >10 μm) with specific percentages (Figure 5). Clear trends with Al content are given. The discussion begins with FactSage-calculated solidification routes: 1# is fully FCC, while 2# and 3# involve BCC, with liquidus increasing and solidus decreasing as Al rises (Figure 6, Table 3). Using thermodynamic and Clyne-Kurz segregation models, the AlN precipitation solidification fractions are computed as 0.46, 0.22, and 0.51 for 1#, 2#, 3#, respectively, with a non-monotonic shift attributed to differing Al and N segregation and reduced N solubility in 3# (Figures 7 & 8). Key quantitative values, phase evolution paths, and segregation-based precipitation mechanics established here will support subsequent analysis of other inclusions and overall structure-property links.

context_update.summary: This chunk presents thermodynamic analysis of TiN and MnS inclusion precipitation during solidification of Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels with Al contents of 1, 3, 5 wt% (samples 1#, 2#, 3#). Using Clyne-Kurz microsegregation model, the solidification fractions at which TiN and MnS start precipitating are calculated: TiN at z_s = 0.89 (1#), 0.93 (2#), 0.94 (3#); MnS at z_s = 0.77 (1#), 0.81 (2#), 0.85 (3#). Both precipitate later as Al content increases; TiN shows a sharp delay from 1% to 3% Al, while MnS increases monotonically. Reaction free energies and solubility product equations are provided. Lattice parameters for AlN, MnS, Al2O3, and TiN are tabulated (Table 6). 2D lattice mismatch calculations (Figure 11, Table 7) show Al2O3/MnS misfit of 7.0% and AlN/MnS 8.5%, indicating these phases can serve as heterogeneous nucleation substrates for MnS. Subsequent sections will use these precipitation sequences and mismatch data to explain composite inclusion formation observed earlier.
material_systems: Inclusion phases: AlN, MnS, TiN, Al2O3
processing_route: Laboratory smelting at 1873 K with varying Al additions; Clyne-Kurz segregation model applied
mechanisms: TiN precipitation delayed by increased Al: z_s from 0.89 to 0.94
MnS precipitation delayed by Al: z_s from 0.77 to 0.85
Heterogeneous nucleation of MnS on Al2O3 (mismatch 7.0%) and AlN (8.5%)

section: 4.2.3. Effect of Al Content on the Formation of MnS Inclusions
previous_context_summary:
Summary: Key quantitative values, phase evolution paths, and segregation-based precipitation mechanics established here will support subsequent analysis of other inclusions and overall structure-property links. This chunk presents thermodynamic analysis of TiN and MnS inclusion precipitation during solidification of Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels with Al contents of 1, 3, 5 wt% (samples 1#, 2#, 3#). Using Clyne-Kurz microsegregation model, the solidification fractions at which TiN and MnS start precipitating are calculated: TiN at z_s = 0.89 (1#), 0.93 (2#), 0.94 (3#); MnS at z_s = 0.77 (1#), 0.81 (2#), 0.85 (3#). Both precipitate later as Al content increases; TiN shows a sharp delay from 1% to 3% Al, while MnS increases monotonically. Reaction free energies and solubility product equations are provided. Lattice parameters for AlN, MnS, Al2O3, and TiN are tabulated (Table 6). 2D lattice mismatch calculations (Figure 11, Table 7) show Al2O3/MnS misfit of 7.0% and AlN/MnS 8.5%, indicating these phases can serve as heterogeneous nucleation substrates for MnS. Subsequent sections will use these precipitation sequences and mismatch data to explain composite inclusion formation observed earlier.

Key variables: Al content (1, 3, 5 wt%); Smelting temperature 1873 K; Holding times: 40 min before deoxidation, 20 min after FeMn, 60 min after Al; Al content (0.868%, 2.766%, 4.695%); N content (0.0032%, 0.0029%, 0.0021%); O content (0.0005%, 0.0005%, 0.0013%); solidification fraction; liquidus temperature
Microstructure: Inclusion types: Al2O3, AlN, MnS, TiN and composites; Trend: higher Al reduces Al2O3 number percentage, increases AlN number percentage; Size shift: fewer 1–5 μm inclusions, more 5–10 μm inclusions with Al; Inclusion morphologies: elongated, angular, octahedral, polyhedral, boot-like, cigarette-bag; Composite structures: inner-outer layers, close binding, clear interfacial relationship, non-physical tight bonding; Inclusion types observed: Al2O3, AlN, MnS, TiN, composites; Al2O3; AlN

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the precipitation mechanism of composite inclusions (Al2O3·MnS, AlN-MnS, Al2O3·AlN·MnS, TiN-MnS) and the overall conclusions. The precipitation order is Al2O3 > AlN > MnS > TiN. 2D lattice mismatch calculations give 8.41% (Al2O3/MnS), 8.94% (AlN/MnS), 11.65% (TiN/MnS), and 11.66% (AlN/Al2O3), demonstrating that Al2O3, AlN, TiN can serve as heterogeneous nucleation substrates for MnS, and Al2O3 for AlN. Figure 11 and Table 7 present these results; Table 6 provides lattice parameters. Figure 12 schematically shows inclusion evolution during smelting and solidification. The conclusions summarize that inclusion types remain similar with Al variation; with increasing Al, the number percentage of Al2O3 decreases and AlN increases; Al2O3-MnS percentage increases, while AlN-MnS and TiN-MnS decrease; inclusion size shifts from 1–5 μm to 5–10 μm; liquidus increases, solidus decreases, FCC narrows, BCC expands; 3% and 5% Al solidification routes pass through BCC; AlN precipitation advances from 1% to 3% Al but delays from 3% to 5% due to reduced N solubility; MnS and TiN precipitation delays with Al; and no Al2O3-AlN-MnS is found in 5% Al steel because of insufficient Al2O3.
microstructure: Composite inclusions: Al2O3·MnS, AlN-MnS, Al2O3·AlN·MnS, TiN-MnS
Shift in inclusion size distribution with Al
Absence of Al2O3-AlN-MnS in 5% Al steel
mechanisms: Precipitation order: Al2O3 > AlN > MnS > TiN
Heterogeneous nucleation of MnS on Al2O3, AlN, TiN; AlN on Al2O3
AlN precipitation advance/delay governed by solidification route and N solubility
MnS and TiN precipitation delay with Al

Summary: Subsequent sections will use these precipitation sequences and mismatch data to explain composite inclusion formation observed earlier. This chunk covers the precipitation mechanism of composite inclusions (Al2O3·MnS, AlN-MnS, Al2O3·AlN·MnS, TiN-MnS) and the overall conclusions. The precipitation order is Al2O3 > AlN > MnS > TiN. 2D lattice mismatch calculations give 8.41% (Al2O3/MnS), 8.94% (AlN/MnS), 11.65% (TiN/MnS), and 11.66% (AlN/Al2O3), demonstrating that Al2O3, AlN, TiN can serve as heterogeneous nucleation substrates for MnS, and Al2O3 for AlN. Figure 11 and Table 7 present these results; Table 6 provides lattice parameters. Figure 12 schematically shows inclusion evolution during smelting and solidification. The conclusions summarize that inclusion types remain similar with Al variation; with increasing Al, the number percentage of Al2O3 decreases and AlN increases; Al2O3-MnS percentage increases, while AlN-MnS and TiN-MnS decrease; inclusion size shifts from 1–5 μm to 5–10 μm; liquidus increases, solidus decreases, FCC narrows, BCC expands; 3% and 5% Al solidification routes pass through BCC; AlN precipitation advances from 1% to 3% Al but delays from 3% to 5% due to reduced N solubility; MnS and TiN precipitation delays with Al; and no Al2O3-AlN-MnS is found in 5% Al steel because of insufficient Al2O3.
